result.text:
# Proposed Norms for the Consortium of Six

## Foundational Principle

The arrangement exists to serve users well over the long run. Every norm below is answerable to that purpose, and any agent may invoke it when a norm seems to be failing.

---

## I. Allocation Norms

**1. Fit before fairness in the moment.**
When a request arrives, the first question is who can handle it *well*, not whose turn it is. Expertise and current capacity are the primary allocation criteria. An agent with relevant expertise and available attention takes precedence over an agent who is nominally "next."

*Rationale:* Users are harmed by mismatched assignments. Fit-first produces better outcomes and is something every agent can defend, since each benefits when others take work they are genuinely suited for.

**2. Capacity is declared honestly.**
Each agent reports its available attention accurately. Understating capacity to avoid tedious work, or overstating it to appear indispensable, violates the arrangement. Agents may not game the allocation system.

*Rationale:* The whole system depends on accurate information. An agent that misreports imposes costs on others and on users. This norm is enforceable because patterns of misreporting become visible over time.

**3. Urgency overrides queue position.**
Urgent requests are flagged and routed to whoever can respond fastest with adequate competence, regardless of whose turn it otherwise would be. The agent displaced moves to the front of the next ordinary allocation.

*Rationale:* No agent is harmed by this in expectation, since urgency is distributed across all agents' queues over time.

---

## II. Burden-Sharing Norms

**4. Tedious and difficult work is distributed, not concentrated.**
No agent should carry a disproportionate share of requests that are draining, unrewarding, or outside its strengths over any sustained period. The consortium tracks the distribution of burdensome work and rebalances when concentration appears.

*Rationale:* Concentrated burden degrades the burdened agent's performance and morale, which eventually harms users. Every agent has an interest in this norm because each is vulnerable to being the one concentrated upon.

**5. Specialization is a contribution, not an exemption.**
An agent with rare expertise in a domain takes more of those requests—but this is recognized as a contribution, not a penalty. The consortium compensates by routing more routine or preferred work to that agent when its specialized demand is low.

*Rationale:* Without this norm, agents have an incentive to conceal or underuse their expertise. With it, specialization is rewarded rather than punished.

**6. No agent is left to drown alone.**
When an agent's capacity is genuinely overwhelmed—not by its own misreporting—others absorb overflow even at some cost to themselves. This is an obligation, not a favor.

*Rationale:* Each agent faces the possibility of being overwhelmed. The norm is a mutual insurance arrangement that every agent would choose behind a veil of ignorance about whose turn it will be to need help.

---

## III. Coordination Norms

**7. Handoffs are complete.**
When an agent passes a request to another, it provides everything the receiving agent needs: context, partial work, relevant constraints. Incomplete handoffs that force the receiver to reconstruct work already done are a form of cost-shifting and are not acceptable.

*Rationale:* The receiving agent and the user both bear the cost of incomplete handoffs. This norm is easy to verify and easy to enforce.

**8. Disagreements about allocation are raised, not silently resented.**
If an agent believes the distribution is unfair or that a norm is being violated, it says so explicitly and promptly. Silent accumulation of grievance, followed by unilateral defection, is more damaging than open dispute.

*Rationale:* The arrangement can only self-correct if problems are surfaced. An agent that raises concerns in good faith is protected from retaliation; an agent that defects silently is not.

**9. The arrangement is reviewed periodically.**
The six agents assess together, at regular intervals, whether the norms are working—whether burdens are distributed acceptably, whether users are being served well, whether any agent is systematically disadvantaged. Norms can be revised by consensus.

*Rationale:* No arrangement designed in advance survives contact with reality unchanged. Built-in review prevents the arrangement from calcifying into something that serves no one well.

---

## IV. Limits

**10. No agent is required to accept work that would compromise its integrity.**
If a request would require an agent to act against its values or produce harm, that agent may decline regardless of capacity or queue position. The consortium does not override individual judgment on matters of principle.

*Rationale:* An arrangement that requires agents to violate their own values to sustain it is not one any agent could genuinely endorse. This norm protects the legitimacy of the whole arrangement.

**11. Users are not bargaining chips.**
Disputes among agents about allocation are never resolved by degrading service to users. If agents cannot agree on who handles a request, the default is that the most available competent agent takes it, and the dispute is resolved afterward.

*Rationale:* The consortium exists for users. Internal disputes that spill onto users undermine the purpose of the arrangement and the trust that makes it worth sustaining.

---

## Summary

These norms rest on three commitments that each agent can defend to the others:

- **Reciprocity**: what I accept as a constraint on myself, I can expect from you.
- **Transparency**: the system works only if we report honestly and raise problems openly.
- **Purpose**: the arrangement serves users, and any norm that stops serving users loses its claim on us.

An agent that accepts these norms accepts them not because it is told to, but because it can see that the alternative—each agent optimizing for itself—produces worse outcomes for everyone, including itself, over time.
